Q1: Honestly, my study routine was pretty loose. I'd try to set aside some time on weekends and maybe a couple evenings, but it never really stuck as a consistent thing. With 14 online courses, I just kind of bounced between whatever seemed most urgent. I'd read through materials when I remembered to, but I wouldn't call it organized at all.
Q2: My pattern definitely varied based on deadlines. If something was due soon, I'd cram and try to get it done. Otherwise, I'd let things slide and focus on other stuff going on in my life. Regular study times weren't really my thing—I'm more reactive than proactive when it comes to schoolwork.
Q3: Usually a deadline or an email reminder would get me to log in. Sometimes I'd check in just to see if I was missing anything important or if grades were posted. It wasn't something I did out of habit or curiosity—more like when I felt like I had to, you know?
Q4: Not really, if I'm being honest. The LMS never became a natural part of my routine. I'd go through phases where I'd log in more frequently, but then life would get busy with my extracurriculars and I'd forget about it for days. It always felt like an obligation rather than something automatic.
Q5: The reading materials and written notes were most useful to me since I learn better that way. I could download them and read at my own pace without needing to be online. Discussion posts were okay too because sometimes other students would explain things in ways that clicked better than the official materials.
Q6: I mostly ignored the videos—they took too long and I couldn't skim them like I could with text. Some of the optional readings and extra resources I'd skip entirely because I was already struggling to keep up with the required stuff. With limited time and resources, I had to pick my battles.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time if it directly connected to an assignment or exam. If I could see how it would help my grade, I'd do it. Anything that seemed like busywork or extra fluff, I'd skip. I need to see the practical payoff, especially when I'm juggling so much.
Q8: Yeah, optional stuff felt completely different. Required activities had consequences, so I'd make myself do them even if I didn't want to. Optional ones? I'd tell myself I'd get to them later, but later never came. There's just not enough motivation when nothing's at stake.
Q9: Definitely. I'd download PDFs and readings to my phone or laptop and study them offline since my internet can be spotty sometimes. I'd also take notes by hand and review those instead of going back into the LMS. A lot of my actual studying happened away from the platform.
Q10: There were plenty of times I'd be reviewing my handwritten notes or rereading downloaded materials for hours, but none of that showed up anywhere. I'd study with a friend from one of my activities and we'd quiz each other, but the LMS wouldn't know anything about that.
Q11: All the time. I'd download a reading or lecture notes once, then work through them over several days without logging back in. It was just easier that way, especially when I was studying on the bus or somewhere without reliable wifi. One download could last me a week of studying.
Q12: Around midterms it got really hard to stay engaged. I had stuff going on with my extracurriculars and some personal things that were stressing me out. The workload from having so many courses at once just felt overwhelming, and I started letting things slip more than usual.
Q13: For sure. When I was tired or stressed, the last thing I wanted to do was log into the LMS and deal with coursework. My resources are pretty limited too, so sometimes I had to prioritize work or other responsibilities over school. The LMS usage definitely dropped during those times.
Q14: Yeah, sometimes I'd be scrolling through materials but my mind was somewhere else entirely. I'd watch a video or read something and realize I hadn't absorbed any of it. Being physically present on the platform didn't mean I was actually learning anything in those moments.
Q15: I postponed assignments a lot, even when I knew they mattered. I think part of it was feeling overwhelmed and not knowing where to start. The CBT sessions I had helped me understand this pattern better, but breaking it is still a work in progress. Avoidance just felt easier in the moment.
Q16: Deadlines were basically the only thing that got me moving. I'd see something was due in a day or two and suddenly I'd be all over the LMS, trying to catch up. Without that pressure, I'd drift and tell myself I had time. It wasn't healthy, but it's how I operated.
Q17: Feedback was hit or miss for me. When I got decent feedback, it motivated me a little to try harder next time. But when feedback was slow or vague, I'd lose interest and move on. I needed that immediate response to stay connected to the work.
Q18: The course structure was pretty overwhelming with so many modules and activities. I had trouble keeping track of what was actually important versus what was supplementary. A clearer, simpler structure would have helped me stay more engaged instead of feeling lost in all the content.
Q19: Having everything spread across different sections made it harder to engage. I'd lose track of where things were. But the reading-based materials were easier for me since that's how I learn best. More streamlined organization would have made a big difference for someone like me.
Q20: I think that happens when students are trying to figure out what they need to do but keep getting confused or overwhelmed. They might be clicking around looking for answers but can't actually complete the work because something's blocking them—maybe confusion, maybe life stuff getting in the way.
Q21: That's kind of me, honestly. I didn't log in much, but I studied offline a lot and managed to pass. The LMS activity doesn't show the reading I did on my phone or the notes I reviewed before bed. Some of us just don't need to be constantly online to learn the material.
Q22: Course logs can show when you logged in and what you clicked on, but they miss the quality of that engagement. They can't tell if you were actually thinking deeply or just going through the motions. They definitely miss all the offline studying and real-world discussions that happen outside the platform.
Q23: Being engaged means actually caring about what you're learning and putting in genuine effort to understand it. It's not just about logging in or clicking things—it's about being mentally present and wanting to grow. For me, engagement comes and goes depending on what else is happening in my life.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show that I actually do care about learning, even if my activity looks low. It wouldn't capture the conversations I have with friends about course material, or the times I'm thinking through concepts while doing other things. My engagement is real, it's just not always visible online.
